_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Mass extending off the caudal end of the fetus in utero
External sacral mass present at birth when a large tumor is detected protruding from the sacral region (could be asymptomatic or present with signs of obstruction of the rectum or bladder)
mucin discharge from a sinus hidden within the anal corrugations

preauth.label: Serum levels of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and beta human chorionic gonadotropin (beta-hCG)

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure?
Serum levels of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and beta human chorionic gonadotropin (beta-hCG)?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge available?
Was the CT/MRI report (grading the Sacrococcygeal teratoma) submitted?

common_queries: Patient did not present with external sacral mass or intraabdominal palpable mass as indicated in the clinical notes.
CT/MRI of the primary site or serum levels of AFP/beta-hCG did not confirm sacrococcygeal teratoma as per submitted reports.
Histopathological report was not submitted at claim stage.
Clinical notes are missing or lacking detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure at pre-authorization.
CT/MRI of the primary site report was not provided at pre-authorization.
Serum levels of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and beta human chorionic gonadotropin (beta-hCG) reports were not provided at pre-authorization.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) with daily vitals and line of treatment are not available at claim stage.
Detailed procedure / Operative Notes are not available at claim stage.
Discharge summary with follow-up advice is not available at claim stage.

clinical_key_pointers: Sacrococcygeal teratoma (SCT) is the most common germ cell tumor in infancy and early childhood.
Malignant elements can be present, and their frequency increases with the postnatal age of the patient.
Sacrococcygeal teratomas are classified according to the American Academy of Pediatrics Surgical Section:
Type I - the tumor is predominantly external with a very minimal internal component. Type I is rarely associated with malignancy.
Type II - the tumor is predominantly external but has some internal extension into the presacral space.
Type III – the tumor is visible externally but is predominantly located in the pelvic area with some extension into the abdomen.
Type IV - the tumor is not visible externally and is located in the presacral space. Type IV has the highest rate of malignancy.
surgical_options: In most cases, surgical resection is undertaken postnatally
Resection always involves resection of the tumor along with the coccyx
Surgical excision could be of local (inverted V-shaped or Chevron or posterior sagittal incision) or abdominosacral approach
Abdominosacral approach for lesions palpated per abdomen
Sacral route with posterior sagittal incision for tumors with size <5 cm and located more in the midline.
Sacral route with inverted V-shaped or Chevron incision for larger or eccentrically located masses
